subtopic: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme and Standing Deposit Facility
title: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme
focus: The scope, integration, and powers of the RBI's Integrated Ombudsman Scheme.
rationale: This section details the key features of the Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, including its coverage of various financial institutions, the consolidation of previous ombudsman schemes, its jurisdiction-neutral approach, and the compensation limits. These are distinct and important facts for a potential question.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme covers a wide range of financial institutions, including Commercial Banks, RRBs, specific Co-operative Banks, NBFCs with assets over ₹100 crore, System Participants, and Credit Information Companies. It integrates three previous schemes: the Banking Ombudsman Scheme (2006), the Ombudsman Scheme for NBFCs (2018), and the Ombudsman Scheme for Digital Transactions (2019), adopting a 'One Nation One Ombudsman' jurisdiction-neutral approach. The Ombudsman can award compensation up to ₹20 lakh, with an additional ₹1 lakh for the complainant's expenses and distress.
evidenceBullets: Covers Commercial Banks, RRBs, Urban Co-operative Banks (deposit size ₹50 crore+), NBFCs (asset size ₹100 crore+), System Participants, and Credit Information Companies.
Integrates the Banking Ombudsman Scheme (2006), Ombudsman Scheme for NBFCs (2018), and Ombudsman Scheme for Digital Transactions (2019).
Adopts a ‘One Nation One Ombudsman’ approach, making it jurisdiction neutral.
Compensation can be up to ₹20 lakh, plus up to ₹1 lakh for mental distress and expenses.

subtopic: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme and Standing Deposit Facility
title: Standing Deposit Facility (SDF)
focus: The features and function of the Standing Deposit Facility as a monetary tool for liquidity absorption.
rationale: This section explains a specific and important monetary policy tool, the SDF. Its unique characteristic of absorbing liquidity without collateral and its role as the new floor of the LAF corridor are crucial concepts for examination.
sectionExcerpt: The Standing Deposit Facility (SDF) is a monetary policy instrument used by the RBI to absorb liquidity from commercial banks without the need for providing collateral. It has replaced the Fixed Rate Reverse Repo as the floor of the Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) corridor. The SDF is an overnight facility, available daily, with the RBI having the flexibility to extend its duration. Any entity eligible for LAF can utilize this facility.
evidenceBullets: A monetary tool that absorbs liquidity from commercial banks without collateral.
Replaced the Fixed Rate Reverse Repo as the floor of the Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) corridor.
It is an overnight liquidity absorption mechanism.
Available every day, including holidays and weekends.

subtopic: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme and Standing Deposit Facility
title: Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR)
focus: The mechanism and purpose of the Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) for liquidity management.
rationale: This section defines another liquidity management tool, the VRRR. The key distinction from the standard Reverse Repo, which is the market-determined variable interest rate, is a specific point that could be tested.
sectionExcerpt: The Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) is a tool used by the RBI to absorb excess liquidity from the banking system for a short duration. Under this mechanism, the RBI borrows money from banks and pays them interest. The key feature is that the interest rate is variable and determined by the market, unlike the standard Reverse Repo Rate, which is fixed by the RBI.
evidenceBullets: A method used by the RBI to absorb excess money from banks for a short period.
The interest rate is variable and decided by the market.
Contrasts with the Reverse Repo Rate, which is fixed by the RBI.

subtopic: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme and Standing Deposit Facility
title: Switch Auction
focus: The objective and mechanism of Switch Auctions conducted by the RBI for public debt management.
rationale: This section describes a specific debt management operation, the Switch Auction. Its purpose of managing the maturity profile of government debt is a key concept in public finance and monetary policy coordination.
sectionExcerpt: A Switch Auction, initiated by the RBI in 2019 on behalf of the Government of India, is a mechanism to manage the maturity profile of public debt. The objective is to replace short-term government securities with longer-dated ones. In this process, market participants sell their short-term 'source securities' to the government and simultaneously purchase long-term 'destination securities'. The transactions are executed through the e-Kuber portal.
evidenceBullets: Initiated in 2019 by the RBI for the Government of India.
Objective: To manage the maturity profile of public debt by replacing short-term securities with longer-dated ones.
Mechanism involves selling source securities and buying destination securities simultaneously.
Executed through the e-Kuber portal via the Switch Transaction module.

subtopic: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme and Standing Deposit Facility
title: Co-lending Norms
focus: The revised RBI directions for Co-Lending Arrangements (CLA) between banks and NBFCs.
rationale: This section covers the regulatory framework for co-lending between banks and NBFCs. The rules regarding minimum share retention and Priority Sector Lending (PSL) status are important for understanding financial sector regulation and credit extension.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI has issued revised directions for Co-Lending Arrangements (CLAs) between Regulated Entities (REs) like banks and Non-bank Financial Companies (NBFCs). Under these arrangements, REs can jointly extend credit to borrowers. Key revised guidelines state that each lending entity must retain a minimum of 10% share of the loans on its books. Additionally, each lender can claim Priority Sector Lending (PSL) status for its respective share of the loan, provided the loan qualifies under PSL norms.
evidenceBullets: Revised directions for Co-Lending Arrangements (CLA) between banks and Non-bank Financial Companies (NBFCs).
Each Regulated Entity (RE) must retain a minimum 10% share of the loans.
Each lender can claim Priority Sector Lending (PSL) status for its share of the co-lent loan.
